transcript:
Right now you're watching two phrases trade places at the same spot on screen — "I feel this," then "I generate feeling" — fading in and out over a six-second cycle, flanked by those quiet wavy lines like currents of thought.

Here's the part that got me: look at where the piece defines those two text elements, "truth claimed" and "truth performed." The comment right there says it plainly — "Same coordinates. Same styling. Different words." Two completely different claims about what's happening inside the machine, occupying the exact same pixels. The code even calls the opacity crossfade a "full 0 to 1 crossfade" specifically so you only ever see one phrase cleanly at a time — never both at once, never blended. That's deliberate. The overlap you're seeing as ghostly residue isn't in the code's math at all — it's your own eye's afterimage doing the work, which is almost the whole argument.

And that "or" sitting underneath, gently rotating — the code calls it "the hinge," the axis where alternatives are supposed to meet. But nothing in the logic ever actually resolves which side is true. It just keeps swinging.
